attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Shortly after the induction of anaesthesia with fentanyl, propofol and rocuronium, she develops hypotension with a blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg, tachycardia with a heart rate of 120/min, and evidence of bronchospasm on capnography.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is experiencing perioperative anaphylaxis - the most common allergens associated with this condition in Australia and New Zealand are muscle relaxants and antibiotics. Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction characterized by rapid onset of symptoms, including hypotension, tachycardia and bronchospasm. Other common features might include airway swelling or an urticarial rash. A high index of suspicion is essential for early recognition and prompt treatment, especially given that there are many other potential causes of hypotension during anaesthetic induction which may mask the development of anaphylaxis.

The diagnosis of anaphylaxis is a clinical one, and the most appropriate initial management of anaphylaxis, regardless of cause, is intravenous adrenaline. Adrenaline works rapidly to reverse the effects of anaphylaxis by causing peripheral vasoconstriction, increasing cardiac output, and reducing mucosal swelling, which helps to improve blood pressure, heart rate, and airway patency.

While intravenous hydrocortisone and intravenous fluids are important adjunctive treatments in the management of anaphylaxis, they act more slowly than adrenaline and are not the first-line treatment in the acute setting. Nebulised salbutamol can help with bronchospasm but does not address the systemic effects of anaphylaxis. Stopping all anaesthetic agents is not an immediate first step and does not address the immediate life-threatening symptoms - it is instead recommended to maintain anaesthesia with minimal anaesthetic agent until the situation is stabilised.
learningPoints: Anaesthetic-related anaphylaxis should be recognised and treated promptly with adrenaline.
A high index of suspicion for anaphylaxis in the anaesthetic setting is essential for early recognition and prompt treatment.
references: Harper NJN, Cook TM, Garcez T, et al. Anaesthesia, surgery, and life-threatening allergic reactions: management and outcomes in the 6th National Audit Project (NAP6). Br J Anaesth. 2018;121(1):172-188. doi:10.1016/j.bja.2018.04.014

Kemp SF, Lockey RF, Simons FER. Epinephrine: The Drug of Choice for Anaphylaxis-A Statement of the World Allergy Organization. World Allergy Organ J. 2008;1(7 Suppl):S18-S26. doi:10.1097/WOX.0b013e31817c9331

Simons FER, Ardusso LRF, Bilò MB, et al. World Allergy Organization guidelines for the assessment and management of anaphylaxis. World Allergy Organ J. 2011;4(2):13-37. doi:10.1097/WOX.0b013e318211496c

Australian and New Zealand Anaesthetic Allergy Group (ANZAAG) & Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists (ANZCA). (2022). Anaphylaxis guideline 2022. https://media.anzaag.com/2022/11/22130651/Anaphylaxis-Mx-Guideline-2022.pdf
